items.question: When would you use a for loop versus a while loop?
items.answer: Use a for loop when you iterate over a known iterable or a fixed count (items, range, a stream). Use a while loop when the number of repetitions is not known in advance and depends on a runtime condition, such as retrying until success or reading until a stream ends.

items.question: What does the else clause on a loop do?
items.answer: The loop's else block runs only if the loop completed normally without hitting a break. It is commonly used in search loops: the for/while finds an item and breaks, and the else handles the 'not found' case.

items.question: What is the difference between break and continue?
items.answer: break exits the nearest enclosing loop entirely. continue stops the current iteration and jumps to the next one, leaving the loop running. break ends the repetition; continue just skips ahead.

items.question: What makes an object iterable, and what can you loop over?
items.answer: An iterable is anything a for loop can traverse — it provides items one at a time (technically via the iterator protocol). Built-in iterables include lists, tuples, strings, dicts, sets, range objects, files and generators.

items.question: When is match/case a better choice than a chain of if/elif?
items.answer: match/case (3.10+) shines when you branch on the shape or value of one subject — especially when destructuring data (tuples, dicts, classes). For simple boolean conditions, if/elif is fine; for many value/structure cases, match is clearer and includes case _ as an explicit default.
